response: The internet’s origins trace back to the 1960s, when the US Department of Defense funded ARPANET, first demonstrating a networked message between UCLA and the Stanford Research Institute in 1969 and gradually linking universities through the 1970s. The shift to TCP/IP in 1983 laid the technical foundation for today’s internet, and Tim Berners-Lee’s 1989 invention of the World Wide Web—paired with the 1993 launch of the Mosaic browser—made online information accessible to everyday users. The late-1990s dot-com boom and its 2001 crash wiped out many startups but left survivors like Amazon and Google, which went on to become industry titans. The mid-2000s rise of social media platforms, the 2007 advent of the iPhone-driven mobile internet, and the emergence of cloud computing (with AWS debuting in 2006) have since connected over 5 billion people and reshaped commerce, communication, and entertainment.
